title: Jasminum multiflorum
what_description: Jasminum multiflorum is a flowering plant species belonging to the family Oleaceae. It is an evergreen climbing shrub with woody stems, simple, alternate, and elliptical leaves that are 4-10 cm long and 1.5-4 cm wide, with entire margins and a petiole 1-2 cm long. The leaves are dark green, leathery, and have a glossy upper surface. Jasminum multiflorum produces fragrant, white, star-shaped flowers in clusters, 7-10 cm in diameter, with five petals and a calyx of five sepals, in the axils of the leaves from spring to summer. The flowers are followed by red or purple berries that are 1-2 cm in diameter and contain several seeds.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Jasminum multiflorum are anxiety and insomnia, which can be alleviated due to its adaptogenic properties that help regulate the body's response to stress. Digestive issues are also effectively managed with this plant, as it exhibits antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities that soothe the digestive tract. Jasminum multiflorum has been shown to lower hypertension by reducing the levels of stress hormones in the body, thereby promoting cardiovascular health. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties also help alleviate inflammation, pain, and headaches, while also treating skin conditions such as acne and eczema by reducing inflammation and promoting wound healing. Furthermore, this plant has been used to treat menopause symptoms, reduce muscle tension, and combat fatigue, thereby promoting a balanced and healthy lifestyle.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Jasminum multiflorum are rooted in its antioxidant activity, which helps protect the plant's biomass from oxidative damage and may have implications for human health. This plant exhibits antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of various pathogens, including bacteria and fungi, likely due to the presence of flavonoids and saponins. The anti-inflammatory effects of Jasminum multiflorum may be attributed to its content of volatile oils, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate pain. The analgesic properties of this plant are thought to be mediated by the glycosides present in its leaves, which may interact with opioid receptors to produce pain relief. Additionally, the cardioprotective effects of Jasminum multiflorum are believed to be the result of its flavonoid content, which may help mitigate cardiovascular disease by reducing oxidative stress and improving lipid profiles.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Jasminum multiflorum are flavonoids such as quercetin kaempferol and isorhapontigenin which exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties due to their ability to scavenge free radicals and inhibit pro-inflammatory enzymes. Saponins present in the plant have been found to possess antimicrobial and antiviral activities which help in wound healing and protection against infections. Phenolic acids like ferulic and sinapic acids are responsible for the plant's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects and have been reported to exhibit anti-cancer properties. Coumarins such as scopoletin have been found to exhibit anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties which aid in wound healing and protection against infections. Alkaloids present in the plant have been reported to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities which help in wound healing and protection against infections.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Jasminum multiflorum are tincture, tea, infusion, infused oil, and Jasminum tea. A tincture of Jasminum multiflorum is prepared by steeping the flowers in a solvent, typically ethanol or glycerin, to extract its bioactive compounds, which are then used to treat various health issues. The tea is prepared by infusing the flowers in hot water, which is rich in flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins that are responsible for its medicinal properties. Infused oil is prepared by steeping the flowers in a carrier oil, such as coconut or olive oil, to obtain a topical remedy for skin issues. Jasminum tea is a popular preparation made by infusing the flowers in hot water and is used to treat anxiety, insomnia, and other stress-related disorders.
side_effects_description: The possible side effect of using Jasminum multiflorum are experience nausea and cause diarrhea due to its toxic compounds, including flavonoids and alkaloids, which can affect the digestive system and cause gastrointestinal upset. Induce vomiting is also a possible side effect as the body tries to eliminate the toxins. Developing allergic reactions is another potential side effect, as some people may be sensitive to the plant's extracts, leading to symptoms such as hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Triggering allergic reactions in individuals who are already allergic to similar plants is also a concern, as cross-reactivity can occur. These side effects can occur when Jasminum multiflorum is used improperly, such as ingesting large amounts or using it as a medicinal remedy without proper medical supervision.
uses: Jasminum multiflorum is a flowering plant native to the Himalayan region and parts of China, and it has been utilized in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's flowers, leaves, and roots are used in herbal remedies to treat a range of health issues. In traditional Ayurvedic medicine, the plant is used to treat conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory problems. The plant's flowers are also used to treat digestive issues and promote relaxation. Additionally, the plant's roots are used to treat skin conditions and promote wound healing.
taxonomy: Jasminum multiflorum is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae. It is native to South Asia. The classification of Jasminum multiflorum is a part of the larger group of plants known as flowering plants or angiosperms. Further, it is a member of the genus Jasminum, which consists of several species of flowering plants. This species is commonly known as the many-flowered jasmine or the Bengal jasmine.
benefits: Jasminum multiflorum is known to have several health benefits, including its potential to reduce stress and anxiety due to its calming properties. It is also believed to have anti-inflammatory effects, which may help alleviate symptoms of conditions such as arthritis. The plant's fragrance has been used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory issues like bronchitis and coughs. Additionally, its antioxidants may help protect against cell damage and oxidative stress. Furthermore, some studies suggest that it may have a positive impact on cognitive function and memory.
parts: Jasminum multiflorum is a species of flowering plant in the family Oleaceae. The flowers and leaves of Jasminum multiflorum are used in traditional medicine, particularly in Ayurveda and Unani systems. In these traditional medicine systems, the flowers and leaves are used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The plant is also used to treat digestive problems and is believed to have antiseptic properties. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of using Jasminum multiflorum in modern medicine.
intro: Jasminum multiflorum, commonly known as the star jasmine or many-flowered jasmine, is a climbing evergreen shrub native to the Himalayas and southern China, characterized by its star-shaped flowers and strong, sweet fragrance. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for its numerous health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which have been shown to aid in the treatment of various ailments such as fever, digestive issues, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Jasminum multiflorum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Jasminum multiflorum is often used to make teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being incorporated into various Ayurvedic formulations, such as ghee and oil preparations, to promote overall well-being and health.
